text: So the impetus of all of this, and this has been something that's been on my mind for a long time, which is that I've always cared about climate change, but it was never quite so real until the moment that my son was born about five years ago.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I was like, okay, if the world goes to hell in a handbasket, I've had a very lucky, privileged life.

text: And that's great and all.

text: Maybe I'll live...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: to 50 or 60, and then everything goes terribly wrong.

text: And that's all right.

text: No, I mean, I think my whole theory when switching a job, when switching tech stacks, was that the nuts and bolts of day-to-day programming, like knowing how a constructor works in one language versus another, knowing what kind of frameworks to use or how to use them correctly, that's just a small part of your job.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's so many other elements to it, such as communicating with design and product and figuring out what you're actually going to build.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Just architecting the solution that you're looking for, like how do you build it in a way that's maintainable and that actually works?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Being able to debug code quickly or like, I don't know, there's so many skills, especially with like being a senior developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Those sort of skills that you get don't have as much to do with like the day-to-day nitty-gritty of coding.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: They have more to do with like interpersonal relationships and reviewing code and mentoring and stuff like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I figured all of that would transfer wherever I was going.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And he alluded to something very similar.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I think what he said was, software is just a tool to help accomplish something for people.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, honestly, it was it was sort of a years long process where, like I said, early on wanting to get into climate stuff, I started volunteering.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so that was like gathering some information from like I volunteered to work on a carbon footprinting app for a while, which is part of why I became disillusioned with it, because I think like I was working on this in my spare time.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I didn't want to use it.

text: And it was like, well, if I care this much that I'm writing this app, and I'm not footprinting myself daily, then why would someone else do that?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then in future years from that, I went to classes and learned more about climate and change in general and kind of like figuring out where I'd want to fit myself in the solution.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So there was a lot, there was like years of me just kind of throwing these ideas around.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Some of them would be required.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Some of them would be optional, but like, and some of them would be like, you know, if it happens, great, but whatever.
